title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: recipe dsFlowerClient composition four building blocks: Task: classification regression Model: learning algorithm hyperparameters Strategy: server aggregates updates nodes Privacy: whether apply differential privacy block independent S3 object. can mix match freely. recipe just specification. Nothing runs call ds.flower.run.start().

previous_headings: Available models
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: solid default binary classification. Converges fast federated settings model small (one weight per feature).
code: model_lr <- ds.flower.model.sklearn_logreg(   penalty  = "l2",   C        = 1.0,   max_iter = 200L ) model_lr #> dsflower_model: sklearn_logreg ( sklearn ) #>    penalty = "l2"  #>    C = 1  #>    max_iter = 200L

previous_headings: Available models
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: Supports several loss functions learning rate schedules. "log_loss" loss function gives logistic regression SGD optimization.
code: model_sgd <- ds.flower.model.sklearn_sgd(   loss        = "log_loss",   alpha       = 0.0001,   lr_schedule = "optimal" ) model_sgd #> dsflower_model: sklearn_sgd ( sklearn ) #>    loss = "log_loss"  #>    alpha = 1e-04  #>    lr_schedule = "optimal"

previous_headings: Available models
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: L2-regularized linear classifier. Good high-dimensional data.
code: model_ridge <- ds.flower.model.sklearn_ridge(alpha = 1.0) model_ridge #> dsflower_model: sklearn_ridge ( sklearn ) #>    alpha = 1

previous_headings: Available models
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: problems linear model expressive enough: local_epochs controls many passes local data node makes per round. epochs can speed convergence risk local model drift (especially non-IID data).
code: model_mlp <- ds.flower.model.pytorch_mlp(   hidden_layers = c(128L, 64L),   learning_rate = 0.001,   batch_size    = 32L,   local_epochs  = 2L ) model_mlp #> dsflower_model: pytorch_mlp ( pytorch ) #>    hidden_layers = c(128, 64)  #>    learning_rate = 0.001  #>    batch_size = 32L  #>    local_epochs = 2L

previous_headings: Aggregation strategies
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: standard algorithm. node trains, sends updated weights, server computes weighted average proportional dataset size.
code: strat_avg <- ds.flower.strategy.fedavg(   fraction_fit          = 1.0,   fraction_evaluate     = 1.0,   min_fit_clients       = 2L,   min_available_clients = 2L ) strat_avg #> dsflower_strategy: FedAvg  #>    fraction_fit = 1  #>    fraction_evaluate = 1  #>    min_fit_clients = 2L  #>    min_available_clients = 2L

previous_headings: Aggregation strategies
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: Adds proximal term keep local models closer global model. Helps heterogeneous (non-IID) data.
code: strat_prox <- ds.flower.strategy.fedprox(   proximal_mu           = 0.1,   min_fit_clients       = 2L,   min_available_clients = 2L ) strat_prox #> dsflower_strategy: FedProx  #>    proximal_mu = 0.1  #>    fraction_fit = 1  #>    min_fit_clients = 2L  #>    min_available_clients = 2L

previous_headings: Privacy settings
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: Clips gradient norms adds calibrated noise. Smaller epsilon = stronger privacy noisier model.
code: priv_dp <- ds.flower.privacy.dp(   epsilon       = 1.0,   delta         = 1e-5,   clipping_norm = 1.0 ) priv_dp #> dsflower_privacy: dp  #>    epsilon = 1  #>    delta = 1e-05  #>    clipping_norm = 1

title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
code: recipe_cls <- ds.flower.recipe(   task            = ds.flower.task.classification(),   model           = model_lr,   strategy        = strat_avg,   privacy         = ds.flower.privacy.research(),   num_rounds      = 10L,   target_column   = "diagnosis",   feature_columns = c("age", "bmi", "glucose", "insulin") ) recipe_cls #> dsflower_recipe #>   Task:      classification  #>   Model:     sklearn_logreg ( sklearn ) #>   Strategy:  FedAvg  #>   Privacy:   research  #>   Rounds:    10  #>   Target:    diagnosis  #>   Features:  age, bmi, glucose, insulin recipe_reg <- ds.flower.recipe(   task            = ds.flower.task.regression(),   model           = ds.flower.model.sklearn_sgd(loss = "squared_error"),   strategy        = strat_avg,   num_rounds      = 10L,   target_column   = "price",   feature_columns = c("sqft", "bedrooms", "bathrooms") ) recipe_reg #> dsflower_recipe #>   Task:      regression  #>   Model:     sklearn_sgd ( sklearn ) #>   Strategy:  FedAvg  #>   Privacy:   research  #>   Rounds:    10  #>   Target:    price  #>   Features:  sqft, bedrooms, bathrooms

what: Under the hood: from recipe to Flower App
title: Composing Experiments with Recipes
text: call ds.flower.run.start(recipe), recipe converted Flower App: .build_flower_app() copies Python template inst/flower_templates/<model_template>/ containing client_app.py (local training) server_app.py (aggregation strategy). .write_pyproject_toml() serializes recipe parameters TOML: flwr run installs app, starts ServerApp, orchestrates training SuperLink.

title: Understanding dsFlower: A Complete Guide
text: patient data three hospitals. want train machine learning model learns three datasets, move data hospital. standard federated learning setup, exactly DataSHIELD designed enable statistical analysis. dsFlower extends DataSHIELD support federated learning, just federated statistics. bridging two systems: DataSHIELD (already know) Flower (Python federated learning framework).

title: Understanding dsFlower: A Complete Guide
text: dsFlower split two R packages run different machines: dsFlowerClient runs laptop. orchestrator. use connect servers, prepare data, start training, collect results. dsFlower runs Opal/Rock server. receives instructions dsFlowerClient, manages local training processes, enforces server administrator’s disclosure controls. never interact directly.

title: Understanding dsFlower: A Complete Guide
text: dsFlower uses two completely separate network channels time: control channel (top) standard DataSHIELD HTTPS. training channel (bottom) Flower’s gRPC protocol. two channels? DataSHIELD’s request-response model support continuous, bidirectional communication federated training needs.

title: Understanding dsFlower: A Complete Guide
text: connecting server, dsFlowerClient lets compose experiment four building blocks. plain R objects describe happen, without triggering computation: combine recipe: recipe just specification. Nothing runs call ds.flower.run.start(). See vignette("experiment-recipes") available models, strategies, privacy settings.
code: library(dsFlowerClient)  # What kind of problem? task <- ds.flower.task.classification() task #> dsflower_task: classification # What model to train? model <- ds.flower.model.sklearn_logreg(C = 1.0, max_iter = 200L) model #> dsflower_model: sklearn_logreg ( sklearn ) #>    penalty = "l2"  #>    C = 1  #>    max_iter = 200L # How to aggregate updates from nodes? strategy <- ds.flower.strategy.fedavg(   fraction_fit          = 1.0,   min_fit_clients       = 3L,   min_available_clients = 3L ) strategy #> dsflower_strategy: FedAvg  #>    fraction_fit = 1  #>    fraction_evaluate = 1  #>    min_fit_clients = 3L  #>    min_available_clients = 3L # Any privacy enhancements? privacy <- ds.flower.privacy.research() privacy #> dsflower_privacy: research recipe <- ds.flower.recipe(   task            = task,   model           = model,   strategy        = strategy,   privacy         = privacy,   num_rounds      = 5L,   target_column   = "target",   feature_columns = c("f1", "f2", "f3", "f4", "f5") ) recipe #> dsflower_recipe #>   Task:      classification  #>   Model:     sklearn_logreg ( sklearn ) #>   Strategy:  FedAvg  #>   Privacy:   research  #>   Rounds:    5  #>   Target:    target  #>   Features:  f1, f2, f3, f4, f5

title: Secure Connections: TLS Auto-Certificates
text: Flower gRPC channel SuperLink (machine) SuperNodes (Opal/Rock servers) carries model parameters, gradients, evaluation metrics. Without encryption, anyone network path : Eavesdrop gradient updates (gradient inversion attacks can leak training data) Redirect SuperNode rogue SuperLink sends back poisoned model Tamper gradient updates transit, degrading model quality DataSHIELD/Opal control channel already HTTPS, Flower gRPC training channel separate. default runs unencrypted. Setting insecure = FALSE enables TLS.

title: Secure Connections: TLS Auto-Certificates
text: insecure = FALSE, dsFlowerClient auto-generates ephemeral TLS certificates using system openssl CLI. Let’s see produces: Seven files. Let’s look important one.
code: library(dsFlowerClient)  cert_dir <- tempfile("dsflower_certs_") certs <- dsFlowerClient:::.generate_tls_certs(cert_dir)  cat("Generated files:\n") #> Generated files: for (f in list.files(cert_dir)) {   sz <- file.size(file.path(cert_dir, f))   cat(sprintf("  %-15s  %d bytes\n", f, sz)) } #>   ca.key           302 bytes #>   ca.pem           444 bytes #>   ca.srl           17 bytes #>   san.cnf          95 bytes #>   server.csr       367 bytes #>   server.key       302 bytes #>   server.pem       538 bytes

previous_headings: How certificate generation works
title: Secure Connections: TLS Auto-Certificates
text: seven steps take roughly 5 milliseconds total (EC P-256 fast): use -extfile san.cnf instead -addext -addext supported LibreSSL (default macOS).
code: 1. Sys.which("openssl")          → find the binary 2. ecparam -name prime256v1      → probe EC support 3. Write san.cnf                 → SAN configuration 4. ecparam -genkey + req -x509   → CA key + self-signed cert (1-day expiry) 5. ecparam -genkey + req + x509  → server key + CSR + sign with CA 6. Sys.chmod("ca.key", "0600")   → restrict permissions 7. readLines("ca.pem")           → load PEM for distribution cat("OpenSSL version:", system2("openssl", "version", stdout = TRUE), "\n") #> OpenSSL version: LibreSSL 3.3.6

title: DataSHIELD Client for Flower Federated Learning
text: dsFlowerClient lets train machine learning models across hospitals without moving patient data. bridges DataSHIELD (privacy-preserving infrastructure) Flower (federated learning framework).

title: DataSHIELD Client for Flower Federated Learning
text: Composable recipes — mix match tasks, models, strategies, privacy settings Multiple models — Logistic Regression, SGD, Ridge, PyTorch MLP Aggregation strategies — FedAvg, FedProx Differential privacy — optional gradient clipping noise Auto-TLS — ephemeral certificates encrypted gRPC channels Server-side controls — disclosure thresholds, round limits, spawn permissions

title: DataSHIELD Client for Flower Federated Learning
code: library(dsFlowerClient) library(DSI) library(DSOpal)  # 1. Connect to Opal servers builder <- DSI::newDSLoginBuilder() builder$append(server = "site_a", url = "https://opal1.example.org",                user = "researcher", password = "secret",                driver = "OpalDriver") builder$append(server = "site_b", url = "https://opal2.example.org",                user = "researcher", password = "secret",                driver = "OpalDriver") conns <- DSI::datashield.login(logins = builder$build(), assign = FALSE)  # 2. Initialize and prepare ds.flower.nodes.init(conns, resource = "project.flower_node") ds.flower.nodes.prepare(conns, target_column = "diagnosis",                         feature_columns = c("age", "bmi", "glucose"))  # 3. Start SuperLink and connect nodes ds.flower.superlink.start(insecure = FALSE)  # TLS enabled ds.flower.nodes.ensure(conns)  # 4. Define and run experiment recipe <- ds.flower.recipe(   task     = ds.flower.task.classification(),   model    = ds.flower.model.sklearn_logreg(C = 1.0),   strategy = ds.flower.strategy.fedavg(min_fit_clients = 2L),   num_rounds      = 10L,   target_column   = "diagnosis",   feature_columns = c("age", "bmi", "glucose") )  result <- ds.flower.run.start(recipe, verbose = TRUE)  # 5. Clean up ds.flower.nodes.cleanup(conns) ds.flower.superlink.stop() DSI::datashield.logout(conns)
